does: This paper applies STAMP (Systems-Theoretic Accident Model and Processes) and STPA (Systems-Theoretic Process Analysis)—established safety methodologies from critical systems engineering—to characterize loss of control in AI systems. The authors develop a structured framework that models AI-containing socio-technical systems as control structures and use STPA's hazard analysis technique to systematically identify causal factors leading to loss of control. The work spans both near-term risks (like rapid self-exfiltration) and longer-term existential concerns, providing practitioners with a concrete methodology to recognize when and how humans may lose meaningful control over AI systems through inadequate feedback, communication failures, or erosion of control mechanisms.
connects: This work bridges safety-critical systems engineering and AI safety research, drawing on decades of STAMP methodology development by Nancy Leveson and colleagues for analyzing accidents in aviation, nuclear power, and other high-risk domains. It responds to growing concerns in the AI safety community about loss of control scenarios, extending prior work that has focused on misalignment and value learning to incorporate systems-theoretic perspectives on control. The paper connects to literature on AI governance, specification gaming, and emergent behavior in complex systems, while bringing formal accident analysis frameworks traditionally applied to well-understood physical systems into the domain of increasingly autonomous AI agents.
enables: This framework enables more systematic identification of control vulnerabilities in AI deployments by providing AI practitioners and safety engineers a structured methodology for hazard analysis. It could support development of monitoring systems and safeguards specifically designed to prevent identified loss of control pathways. The STPA-based approach opens directions for formalizing control requirements in AI systems, designing better feedback loops and human oversight mechanisms, and conducting causal analysis when control incidents occur. Future work could extend this to create standardized taxonomies of loss-of-control hazards, develop automated tools for STPA analysis in AI contexts, and establish governance protocols that apply these insights to increasingly capable systems.
